summary: DHH argues that Elon Musk's Twitter acquisition is the most entertaining spectacle in the world, regardless of outcome. He compares Musk's divisiveness to Trump's but notes the stakes are far lower since it's just a social media platform. DHH contends the world doesn't need Twitter and would likely be better off without it. Therefore, both possible outcomes—Musk fixes Twitter or destroys it—represent a win for everyone else.
key_points: Musk has positioned himself at the intersection of countless cultural trends, making him the most compelling public figure of the moment
The stakes of the Twitter saga are refreshingly low compared to political power struggles, making it guilt-free entertainment
The world doesn't need Twitter and would almost certainly be better off without it
The binary outcome creates an asymmetric bet for the public: a better Twitter or no Twitter are both wins
money_quotes: It's the greatest show on earth, no work of fiction could ever hope to compete.
Nor has anyone been able to play the media like a fiddle with such virtuosity.
This reality show is playing out over something as disposable as a struggling social media website.
The world doesn't need Twitter. In fact, the world would almost certainly be better off without Twitter.
That's what makes this show so guiltlessly entertaining to watch: Whichever the outcome, the world wins.
This is a 44 billion dollar gamble where the rest of us will win whatever the outcome.
key_insight: The Musk-Twitter saga is guilt-free entertainment because both possible outcomes—a reformed platform or its destruction—leave the world better off.
